speaker: interviewer
text: Pincus, can you count, please?

speaker: interviewer
text: Count to 10.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Perfect. You have to keep  it there. Today's date, September 20, 1995. Interview with Pincus Weinstock. Interviewer is Doris Lazarus. The interview is being conducted in Bayside, Wisconsin, USA, in English.

speaker: interviewee
text: Today's date is September 20, 1995. I'm Doris Lazarus. I'm conducting an interview with Pincus Weinstock in his home in Bayside, Wisconsin. Please tell us your name. Pincus Weinstock.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell your name?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-I-N-CU-S, W-E-I-N-S-T-O-C-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pincus, the same-- with a K, was it? P-I-N-K-U-S, W-A-Y-N-S-T-R-C-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you called by any other name? Did you have any nickname?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about a Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: When is your birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: December 6, 1915.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you today?

speaker: interviewer
text: What city were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wolbrom, Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland. Was it a large city or a smaller town?

speaker: interviewee
text: A smaller city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a large Jewish population in that city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too many. Like a small city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in a house or in an apartment?

speaker: interviewee
text: In a house. We didn't have apartments in the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a modern house? Did you have running water?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was not modern. You had to go for water, wintertime, bring the water in the house. It was cold as always.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about electricity?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what did you use?

speaker: interviewee
text: A lamp. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] called it. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. I don't know what they call it, something.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you lived with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: We've got five brothers. I'm the middle one. And the oldest is our sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me who was born first?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, Ruchel, was born first.

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: My oldest brother, Leo, Leibish, Mendel, Pincha, Motel, Yitzrik. Yitzrik still lives in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your older sister, Ruchel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ruchel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she married before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was married. She's got two children, a girl and a boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she survive the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what happened to her?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: With her children?

speaker: interviewee
text: With her children.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about your other brothers? What happened to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: The two oldest brothers, they came out of the camp. And one brother-- the youngest and me-- he was not in a camp. He would have survived, I'm sure. Now, he found out they took me to the concentration camp. Now, he was in a good camp. He could come home for a day. So he came home the same day-- the evening was when they took all the Jews from the ghetto, took  them to Auschwitz. And he went to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Motel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your parents' names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Schlomo, Gittel. Mother's Gittel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your father do before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: He went to markets and sold some stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: In a market?

speaker: interviewee
text: A market, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he sell?

speaker: interviewee
text: Clothes for suits, for all kids of material, new stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he make a good living?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, a hard living, very hard.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about your mother? Did she work outside of the home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She helped Father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you or any of your brothers and sisters used to help your mother in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the house? No, not in the house. We worked in places, we made a little money, but not in the house. We just got one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: You all lived in one room?

speaker: interviewee
text: One room.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you all get along? Did you get along with each other?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we got along, yeah. Well, the oldest brother, right away, when they got out, maybe 15 years, they went in another city. They started working. The second one, the same thing. Me, too-- later I went out, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up, what kind of a school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Hebrew school, not the special, good school. My father didn't let me go. And the regular school, he was worried I'd have to go without a cap. So he was so religious, he didn't let us go in the school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he orthodox?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was very orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he wanted you to go to a Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a bar mitzvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: I just went to, when I was 13, they took me to a temple and they gave me [NON-ENGLISH]. They called me to the Torah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how about afterwards?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was the whole thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any celebration with friends or-- No. No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family go to shul?

speaker: interviewee
text: Almost every day, my father sent to shul.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother make Shabbat dinner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shabbat dinner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember Shabbat in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Oh, yeah, like fish and everything. Not gefilte fish, regular fish. Tzimmes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tzimmes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about challah? Did your mother bake?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother made challah, yeah. The city we was living, you couldn't buy a challah. You have to make it for yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about on the Jewish holidays? Did you remember having relatives and friends at your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just the family-- our family. We didn't have a big home. We couldn't invite people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have relatives that you ever visited in other cities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. The city Sosnowiec.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far away were you from Sosnowiec?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sosnowiec, I would say on mileage, it was about 40 miles-- kilometers, maybe 50, over 50.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: By bus. Sometimes with a horse and wagon, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: With a horse and wagon?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. It took us all night to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you had told me that both of your older brothers left your hometown?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they go? To

speaker: interviewee
text: Sosnowiec. They worked in chocolate-- bakery, chocolate.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were growing up, were you involved in any organizations? Did you belong to a group?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It's a Yiddish organization, Betar.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of organization was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Betar.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it Zionist?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, Jabotinsky.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other hobbies when you were growing up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we played baseball. We played soccer.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you think you wanted to grow up to be? What did you want to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we didn't think too much. We couldn't afford-- it was very hard. If somebody wants to be something, going to college was not easy. To be a professional was very hard. So we didn't think about it. We didn't have the experience, the aspirations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you finish school before the war started?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cheder. It was not much, cheder.

speaker: interviewer
text: So were you working?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland, I was working, and I was doing business myself. I made good money.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a job did you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: I worked in a place where they sold porcelain, glasses, pots. So later, I went on my own.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any anti-Semitism where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Very anti-Semitic, very bad, in Poland especially.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember anything specific?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember they give out a paper called-- I don't know what they called the paper-- against Jews. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I remember in 1936, when the president in Poland died, he was very good-- his name was Pilsudski. He was very good for the Jews. And the Polacks said later, your grandpa died. So it meant they could do now what they wanted. I think much came later from Germany. 1933, I think, the stock market got worse and worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year it was when things started to change for you in your hometown? What year would that have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: What do you mean changed? [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]? It was always anti-Semitic. Maybe it was not so much, later it would get worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about in 1939, when Poland was invaded?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I remember. The Polacks-- Polish people helped to find the Jews that were hiding. They told the Gestapo, the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was later, not '39. Later, when the Jews started knowing what's going on, they started hiding. I think for half a pound, for a kilogram of sugar, they said the Jews are hiding. It was very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did things start to change for you? When did you have to give up your business or leave your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939-- '40, 1940 Already. When the war started, we ran away to another city, to Wolbrom. Because I was in Sosnowiec when the war started. So we were thinking-- people said it'll take a longer time for the Germans to come so far. Meanwhile, they come faster than we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were living in Sosnowiec?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sosnowiec when the war broke out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were you living with?

speaker: interviewee
text: With our parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had all moved?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they all moved.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. And so what happened then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I got a job and not far away. From five miles of Sosnowiec is Katowice. Katowice used to belong once, before the First World War, to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went every day to work. If not, they took us to the concentration camp, to Judenlager. So we worked over there. We got the paper for working. We went every day, and we came home in the evening. You were still with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still with my family, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your father also have to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was a sick man. No. He didn't care what happened, so he didn't work. My brothers worked.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: We took every day to work. In Katowice was a big museum, a new museum. They took it apart. They broke  it. Every stone, they took out. It took years just to do it. [NON-ENGLISH], all kinds of things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's the job that you did?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in charge of you when you were doing this kind of work?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a foreman, a Jewish foreman. So we have to be there every morning and go and come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: But was this forced labor?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this was not forced labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: If not-- my youngest brother, 1939, or beginning of '40, they took him right away to the concentration camp. They said, we have five brothers, one's got to go. See, it was a Jewish-- and the police, they said, if one goes to the concentration camp, the rest will be safe. So it went on and on-- and later, the second one-- was just to hold the people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who made the decision that that was the brother that had to go to the concentration camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was the youngest, so he wants to go. He was 17 years old, I think.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they take him?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he ever come back?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He later went to the concentration camp, when the Judenlager, he went to the concentration camp. He was five years. He lives in Israel right now.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he did survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: He did survive. In the beginning, I also sent packages to the camp with food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think he got those packages?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. You know, it was hard to send money sometimes. My father was smart. He told me to take a piece of soap-- soap that you wash your hand with-- you make a hole and put some money inside, paper money, and lay the cover back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this way, I told him, if I send him a letter, he should take good care of the soap. So he was thinking. He knows what he's doing. In the beginning, later we couldn't do it anymore. So you were working in this factory, and you were still living at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a factory, just a place. Yeah, in Katowice. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you living in Katowice now or in Sosnowiec?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sosnowiec. Also, they took me once-- for three months-- they took me to Breslau. Because the Jewish people, that they send them out to Auschwitz, they have to take out all the furniture, all the food and everything. We worked maybe three months over there. Later, we came back to Sosnowiec.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what exactly what were you doing?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the people-- they sent them away to Auschwitz, the Jewish people, in Breslau. So they left everything in the house-- the food and the furniture, the clothes. So we have to move it out, everything, in trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know anything about Auschwitz at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, at this time, I knew of it. In the beginning, we didn't know. Later we know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they kept you there for three months?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe for three months.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Back home to Sosnowiec, to the ghetto. It was already a ghetto. The ghetto was Srodula. And I went back, and I went, every day, to work, again, back to Katowice.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were now in a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: Srodula.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you still with your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Two families, three families in one room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were the conditions in the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very bad, couldn't be worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there enough food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very hard to get food. So if I worked, I helped something. I brought home something, so I helped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you allowed to go in and out of this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: With permission, I think. You have to have something, yeah. You don't want to go-- you don't want to go out, even. You always worry. You have to wear a Jude arm band when you're Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who was in charge of this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: The name was Merin, Jewish, I think, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there ever any transports out of this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was police-- Jewish police, too, over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember if there were any transports where they would take Jewish people out of the ghetto and send them to camps?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. To the camp? Yeah, they took out to the camp, to Auschwitz. When the people went in this ghetto, they didn't take as many more to Auschwitz. Before, they took plenty of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in this ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not long, maybe two months, something. Later, when I was in Katowice like I told you, I went every day. And they took all the boys that worked over there. They sent them-- they called it [NON-ENGLISH]. They took it, that place. And they sent us away to Kaufering, to the concentration camp-- to Judenlager in the beginning.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what year that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: The year was maybe end of '41 or '42 beginning, something like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: When they sent you away this time, were you with anybody else, with anybody from your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, nobody in the family. Soon, my brother found out. I was the last one at home. And my brother found out they sent me away. And he was at a good camp. So he came home to see the parents. The same day was-- they took all the Jews and he went too.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do in this camp? Were you working?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we have to work every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kinds of work. I mean, to make insulation or something. All kind-- what they need. Every day, you didn't have the same work. Most of the work was that they took you in a place where they cement, the things from the cement ate you up. You couldn't work long over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I got a guy, and I told him, you should help me. So he took me up over there and sent me to another place to work. And how were you treated in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very bad. Very bad. Very bad.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given food?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us not enough food. In the beginning, I got somebody, a girl I knew of from home, she worked in the kitchen. And then, concentration camp, the Judenlager, where she helped them out once in a while for a little bit of food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you able to wear your own clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. In Judenlager, you could wear your clothes. In concentration camps you got stripes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you spend in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: In which camp? In Juden-- I think a year, maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you stay healthy when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was healthy in the beginning. But later, I was really weak. Later, they took out the people in the Jewish concentration camp. So they make a concentration camp. He went in a concentration-- In the beginning, I was feeling better. A couple days-- a couple weeks we didn't have to work. We were just there. So it was better. But later it got worse and worse and worse.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after this camp? Where were you taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This camp? This was Dyhernfurth, Breslau. They took us. I was wearing-- when I was working in Dyhernfurth in concentration camp-- I was wearing shoes, Holland shoes, from wood. So if you didn't have enough food to eat, your feet-- I got swollen up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I cut mine-- I got sores on my legs, didn't come out blood, just came out water. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So I saw-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I will not survive too long with my legs swollen and everything. And not in every concentration camp was, they called it in German a little [NON-ENGLISH], like a little hospital, maybe 20, 30 people or so, it was.

speaker: interviewee
text: I figure, I have no choice. There's two ways. All I'll be-- if I not going over there, it just would kill me. I would not be able to work. The other chance I got, if I go to the [NON-ENGLISH], they will mark my number that I got on my hand. After a couple of months, so many people goes to [NON-ENGLISH], they mark down-- write your number. When they got enough people, they take them away to the crematorium. I knew this before, but I had no choice. See, this time-- if you're in a situation like this, you don't know what to do. So I decided I go in the [NON-ENGLISH]. I went in the [NON-ENGLISH], the little hospital. 10 days I was there. I came out a new person. I  was able to work and everything. So I came out. They took my number. I know what they marked down. After two, three months later, they called my number out. They didn't call us by names, just by the number. Tomorrow morning, you have to be-- there's this place, upper place-- the trucks will be there, and you have to go away with the trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: I got over there. A friend-- he was a foreman-- and I went to him. I told him, listen, did you see that's happened to me? They have to go tomorrow to the transport. Can you help me, something? He said, Pincus, I couldn't help me. If they call me, I have to go, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tomorrow morning, in the morning I went down. I came down. There were other people before that went to work at 6 o'clock in the morning. They told me I should give them the jacket and the things that I eat. The part, I don't know what they called it. They know when I go, so I didn't want to get in is all. They took me about an hour later. We went through two days with traveling cattle. And the cattle trucks nothing and the railroad. And we came out, after two days, to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Auschwitz, they said everybody takes off their jacket and see. Yeah, before they said this, they said, who cannot work? The boss is there. You can go in the trucks. I mean, not boss's trucks. So those that could work, they went over there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I got a friend, I know him good from my city, Sosnowiec. And I know he was not able to work. So I started taking him by the hand to work, to the truck. And I started working with him. He came to me, an SS man. He said to me, go back. Let him go by himself. He didn't tell me more. So I went. I listened to what they told me. We left 45 people. 180 were in this transport. The rest went with the trucks. After a half hour, they told us they should go march to a place we didn't know, but later found out this was a crematorium in the shower place-- to take a shower. So we were there. We were sitting, because it was summer, on the floor. I was looking good-- I mean, feeling good, not bad. And came an SS man again. He told us, take off the shirts. And he looked at us. One he send here, one he sent here. So out of these 45 people, left 17. I was in the lucky of 17. The rest went to the crematorium. I found out when I was taken to take a shower. I met that guy that I know from my city, Sosnowiec. He was my barber, even. He told me, Pincus, you've been the luckiest one. The other ones are not alive anymore. This was in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you first got there, did you have to change your clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to wear a uniform in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: I got a uniform. I came with a uniform.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Did they cut your hair?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they always cut the hair. I was in a camp. They cut me two inches with a razor. In case I run away, people should recognize me, at one camp. You couldn't run away in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: How about a number? Did they put a number on your arm?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they put a number.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year did you get there?

speaker: interviewee
text: '44-- The beginning of '44.
